title: The Wanderings of Paracelsus
subtitle: Theophrastus von Hohenheim on the roads of Europe, 1493–1541
subject: paracelsus
design_note: Alchemical Noir: a life of night flights and burned bridges — the withheld-ending animation does its best work when the road ahead is literally dark. Dashed legs mark the routes we only infer.
provenance_note: Itinerary after Charles Webster, Paracelsus: Medicine, Magic and Mission at the End of Time (2008); Philip Ball, The Devil's Doctor (2006); Walter Pagel, Paracelsus (1958). Paracelsus's own claims of travels to Sweden, Muscovy, Egypt, and the Holy Land during the Wanderjahre are legend or unverifiable and are NOT drawn; legs marked 'inferred' reconstruct the most likely route between attested stations. Dates are approximate where the record is thin.

stops.what: Born near the pilgrim abbey of Einsiedeln, son of a physician; the mountain childhood he later credited with teaching him from the 'book of nature'.
His father takes a post teaching at the Fugger mining school in Carinthia. Ores, furnaces, and miners' diseases: the laboratory education that underwrites everything later.
Claims a doctorate in medicine at Ferrara. No matriculation record survives; the degree is plausible but undocumented — like much of the decade around it.
Surfaces from the undocumented Wanderjahre in Salzburg — and leaves hurriedly after the Peasants' War, suspected of sympathy with the rebels.
stops.source: Webster 2008 (the 1517–24 route is largely his own telling)

stops.what: Buys citizenship and enrols in the surgeons' guild — a rare attempt at settling down. It lasts months.

City physician and university lecturer: teaches in German, admits barber-surgeons, and publicly burns Avicenna's Canon at the St John's Day bonfire. Within a year a lawsuit and his patrons' deaths force a night flight.
First refuge after Basel; writes on the 'French disease' while guest of a sympathetic town clerk.
Challenges the medical establishment over syphilis treatment; the city council, prompted by the Leipzig faculty, bans his printing. The manuscripts pile up unpublished.
Treats the humanist burgomaster Vadianus's circle; writes the Opus Paramirum, the core of his medical theory — matter ruled by the tria prima.
Arrives in a plague town over the Brenner and writes a plague tract for it; the Tyrol years are thinly documented wandering among mining settlements.
Die grosse Wundarznei — the great surgery book — finally gets printed. It is the only major work published in his lifetime, and briefly makes him respectable.
Received by King Ferdinand — the itinerant heretic-physician granted a royal audience, though the promised payment never fully arrives.
Dies at the White Horse Inn on 24 September 1541, aged 47, under the protection of the prince-archbishop. Within decades the manuscripts he could not print become the Paracelsian movement.
